fact.title: Cat
fact.fact: Cats use whiskers to sense nearby spaces and can rotate their ears to track tiny sounds.
fact.food: Cats are meat-eaters and need animal protein in their diet.
fact.stronghold: Domestic cats live with people all over the world, with especially large populations in the United States, China, and Brazil.
learningPoints: Cats sleep for many hours each day.
Their rough tongues help them groom their fur.
A cat can land on its feet by twisting its body in the air.

fact.title: Dog
fact.fact: Dogs have an excellent sense of smell and can learn many words, sounds, and routines from people.
fact.food: Dogs eat a mixed diet that usually includes meat-based foods.
fact.stronghold: Domestic dogs are found worldwide, with very large populations in countries such as the United States, Brazil, and India.
learningPoints: Dogs use body language like tail wagging and ear position to communicate.
Their noses can detect scents far better than human noses.
Many dogs enjoy jobs like herding, guiding, and rescue work.

fact.title: Elephant
fact.fact: Elephants are the largest land animals and use their trunks to smell, drink, grab food, and greet each other.
fact.food: Elephants mostly eat grass, leaves, bark, roots, and fruit.
fact.stronghold: One of the biggest wild elephant strongholds is Botswana in southern Africa.
learningPoints: Their trunks have thousands of muscles.
They live in family groups led by older females.
They help forests by spreading seeds as they travel.

fact.title: Giraffe
fact.fact: Giraffes are the tallest land animals, and their long necks help them reach leaves high in trees.
fact.food: Giraffes mostly eat leaves, buds, and shoots, especially from acacia trees.
fact.stronghold: Large giraffe populations are found in several African countries, including Kenya, Tanzania, and South Africa.
learningPoints: A giraffe’s tongue can be dark blue or purple and very long.
They have powerful kicks to defend themselves.
Each giraffe has a unique patch pattern.

fact.title: Hedgehog
fact.fact: Hedgehogs curl into a tight ball when they feel unsafe, using their spines for protection.
fact.food: Hedgehogs eat insects, worms, and other small creatures.
fact.stronghold: Wild hedgehogs are found across parts of Europe, Asia, and Africa, with Britain being well known for its hedgehog population.
learningPoints: Their spines are stiff hairs made of keratin.
Hedgehogs are usually active at night.
They use smell and hearing to find food.

fact.title: Koala
fact.fact: Koalas sleep for many hours each day because eucalyptus leaves do not give them much energy.
fact.food: Koalas mainly eat eucalyptus leaves.
fact.stronghold: Koalas are found in Australia, especially in the eastern parts of the country.
learningPoints: Koalas are marsupials, so babies grow in a pouch.
Their fingerprints are surprisingly similar to human fingerprints.
They have strong claws for climbing trees.

fact.title: Panda
fact.fact: Giant pandas spend much of their day eating bamboo, even though they belong to the meat-eater group of mammals.
fact.food: Pandas mainly eat bamboo shoots and leaves.
fact.stronghold: Wild giant pandas live in mountain forests in China.
learningPoints: Pandas have a special wrist bone that works like a thumb.
They are strong climbers and good swimmers.
A baby panda is tiny compared with its mother.

fact.title: Penguin
fact.fact: Penguins cannot fly, but they use their wings like flippers to glide quickly through water.
fact.food: Penguins eat fish, krill, and squid.
fact.stronghold: Many penguins live in the Southern Hemisphere, with Antarctica and nearby coasts being famous strongholds.
learningPoints: Their black-and-white feathers help camouflage them in water.
Penguins huddle together to stay warm.
Different penguin species live in very different climates.

fact.title: Rabbit
fact.fact: Rabbits use their large ears to hear danger quickly and to help release body heat.
fact.food: Rabbits eat grasses, leafy plants, and vegetables.
fact.stronghold: Wild rabbits are common in many parts of Europe and are also found in many other regions around the world.
learningPoints: Rabbit teeth keep growing, so chewing helps wear them down.
They can hop quickly and zigzag to escape danger.
Many rabbits live in burrows called warrens.

fact.title: Squirrel
fact.fact: Squirrels hide nuts and seeds in many places, and some forgotten seeds can grow into new plants.
fact.food: Squirrels eat nuts, seeds, fruits, and sometimes insects.
fact.stronghold: Squirrels are found in many countries, with large populations across North America, Europe, and Asia.
learningPoints: Their tails help them balance when climbing and jumping.
Squirrels have strong front teeth for cracking nuts.
Many tree squirrels build nests called dreys.

fact.title: Tiger
fact.fact: Every tiger has a unique stripe pattern, just like each person has unique fingerprints.
fact.food: Tigers are meat-eaters and hunt deer, wild boar, and other animals.
fact.stronghold: India is the strongest country for wild tiger populations today.
learningPoints: Tigers are excellent swimmers.
Their striped coats help them hide in tall grass.
They usually hunt alone.

fact.title: Zebra
fact.fact: No two zebras have exactly the same stripe pattern, and the stripes help confuse predators.
fact.food: Zebras mostly eat grasses and tender plants.
fact.stronghold: Large zebra populations are found in East and southern Africa, including Tanzania and Kenya.
learningPoints: Zebras stay in groups for safety.
Their ears can turn to catch sounds from different directions.
Young zebras can stand soon after they are born.

fact.title: Butterfly Art
fact.fact: Butterfly art often uses bright wings, patterns, and symmetry to create a lively picture.
fact.food: Artists may use paint, paper, markers, or mixed materials to make butterfly-themed art.
fact.stronghold: Butterflies inspire artists and craft projects in classrooms, studios, and homes around the world.
learningPoints: Many butterfly artworks use matching shapes on both sides.
Colorful wing patterns help the picture feel playful and energetic.
Nature is a common inspiration for art.

fact.title: Chalk Art
fact.fact: Chalk art uses soft chalk sticks or chalk pastels to draw colorful pictures on paper or pavement.
fact.food: Chalk, pastel sticks, blending tools, and textured paper are often used for this kind of art.
fact.stronghold: Chalk art is popular in schools, sidewalks, festivals, and art studios.
learningPoints: Chalk can be blended with fingers or tools to create soft color transitions.
Sidewalk chalk art can be large and temporary.
Artists often layer colors to make chalk drawings richer.

fact.title: Clay Art
fact.fact: Clay art is made by shaping soft clay into objects, figures, bowls, or decorations.
fact.food: Clay artists often use clay, sculpting tools, water, and sometimes paint or glaze.
fact.stronghold: Clay art appears in classrooms, pottery studios, and traditional crafts around the world.
learningPoints: Clay can be pinched, rolled, pressed, or carved into shapes.
Some clay pieces are dried or fired to become hard and strong.
Pottery is one of the oldest art forms.

fact.title: Collage Art
fact.fact: Collage art is made by arranging and sticking different pieces together to form one picture.
fact.food: Paper scraps, magazine cutouts, fabric, glue, and stickers are common collage materials.
fact.stronghold: Collage is widely used in classrooms, design work, and creative art projects.
learningPoints: Collage combines many textures, colors, and shapes in one artwork.
Artists can overlap pieces to build layers.
Recycled materials are often used in collage.

fact.title: Crayon Art
fact.fact: Crayon art uses wax crayons to make colorful drawings with bold and friendly textures.
fact.food: Crayons and paper are the main materials, though artists sometimes add watercolor or marker too.
fact.stronghold: Crayon art is especially common in schools, homes, and early art learning activities.
learningPoints: Crayons are easy to layer for stronger colors.
Different hand pressure can make lines lighter or darker.
Crayon resist art can make fun effects with paint over wax.

fact.title: Finger Painting
fact.fact: Finger painting lets artists spread paint with their hands and fingers instead of brushes.
fact.food: Washable paint, thick paper, and aprons are often used for finger painting.
fact.stronghold: Finger painting is popular in children’s art activities and sensory play spaces.
learningPoints: It helps children explore color, texture, and movement.
Hands can make swirls, dots, and broad paint strokes.
Finger painting often focuses on creativity more than perfect lines.

fact.title: Painting
fact.fact: Painting uses color and brushstrokes to create scenes, objects, patterns, or feelings on a surface.
fact.food: Paint, brushes, palettes, canvas, and paper are common painting materials.
fact.stronghold: Paintings are created in studios, schools, galleries, and homes around the world.
learningPoints: Artists can paint with many styles, from realistic to abstract.
Brush size changes the kind of marks an artist can make.
Paintings can tell stories or express moods.

fact.title: Paper Craft
fact.fact: Paper craft uses folded, cut, rolled, or layered paper to make artistic shapes and objects.
fact.food: Colored paper, scissors, glue, and folding tools are common paper craft supplies.
fact.stronghold: Paper crafts are made in classrooms, holiday projects, and art studios across many cultures.
learningPoints: Some paper crafts are flat pictures while others become 3D models.
Origami is a famous paper art based on folding.
Paper crafting helps with planning shapes and careful hand movements.

fact.title: Art Studio
fact.fact: An art studio is a creative workspace where artists draw, paint, sculpt, and experiment with materials.
fact.food: Studios may contain paints, easels, brushes, paper, clay, and many other tools.
fact.stronghold: Art studios are found in schools, homes, shared creative spaces, and professional workshops.
learningPoints: Studios are organized so artists can keep tools and projects nearby.
Different art forms often share the same creative space.
A studio can be large or small as long as it supports making art.

fact.title: Watercolor
fact.fact: Watercolor art uses water-thinned paint to create soft, glowing layers of color.
fact.food: Watercolor paints, brushes, water, and thick paper are key materials for watercolor art.
fact.stronghold: Watercolor painting is used in sketchbooks, classrooms, design work, and fine art around the world.
learningPoints: More water usually makes the color look lighter and more transparent.
Artists often paint from light colors to darker ones.
Watercolor can create gentle gradients and flowing textures.

fact.title: Duck
fact.fact: Ducks have waterproof feathers and webbed feet that help them paddle through water.
fact.food: Ducks eat plants, seeds, insects, and small water animals.
fact.stronghold: Ducks are found around lakes, rivers, wetlands, and coasts in many countries around the world.
learningPoints: They spread oil over their feathers to keep water off.
Some ducks dive underwater while others tip forward to feed.
Ducklings can often swim soon after hatching.

fact.title: Flamingo
fact.fact: Flamingos get their pink color from pigments in the food they eat, such as algae and tiny crustaceans.
fact.food: Flamingos filter-feed on algae, shrimp-like animals, and other tiny organisms.
fact.stronghold: Large flamingo groups are found in parts of Africa, South America, and the Caribbean.
learningPoints: They often stand on one leg to conserve body heat.
Their beaks are shaped for filtering food from water.
Flamingos live in big flocks for safety and nesting.

fact.title: Hummingbird
fact.fact: Hummingbirds can hover in place and flap their wings extremely fast to drink nectar from flowers.
fact.food: Hummingbirds drink nectar and also eat tiny insects for protein.
fact.stronghold: Hummingbirds live only in the Americas, with many species found in Central and South America.
learningPoints: They can fly backward as well as forward.
Their long tongues help them sip nectar deep inside flowers.
Hummingbirds are among the smallest birds in the world.

fact.title: Owl
fact.fact: Owls can turn their heads very far and have excellent night vision for hunting in the dark.
fact.food: Owls eat small mammals, insects, fish, and other birds depending on the species.
fact.stronghold: Owls live on every continent except Antarctica, with many species found across forests and grasslands worldwide.
learningPoints: Their soft feathers help them fly almost silently.
Owls swallow some prey whole and later cough up pellets.
Their ears are placed to help pinpoint where sounds come from.

fact.title: Parrot
fact.fact: Parrots are known for their curved beaks, bright feathers, and ability to copy sounds.
fact.food: Parrots often eat seeds, nuts, fruit, and buds.
fact.stronghold: Many parrot species live in tropical parts of South America, Australia, and Southeast Asia.
learningPoints: Parrots use their strong beaks like a third hand when climbing.
Some parrots can learn to mimic human speech.
They are very social birds and often live in groups.

fact.title: Peacock
fact.fact: Male peafowl, often called peacocks, spread their colorful tail feathers in a huge fan display.
fact.food: Peafowl eat seeds, insects, fruit, and small creatures.
fact.stronghold: Indian peafowl are native to India and nearby parts of South Asia.
learningPoints: The shiny eye-spots on the tail help attract attention.
Peafowl can fly short distances even with long tail feathers.
The female is called a peahen.

fact.title: Penguin
fact.fact: Penguins cannot fly, but they use their wings like flippers to glide quickly through water.
fact.food: Penguins eat fish, krill, and squid.
fact.stronghold: Many penguins live in the Southern Hemisphere, with Antarctica and nearby coasts being famous strongholds.
learningPoints: Their black-and-white feathers help camouflage them in water.
Penguins huddle together to stay warm.
Different penguin species live in very different climates.

fact.title: Sparrow
fact.fact: Sparrows are small, active birds that often live close to people and communicate with many chirps.
fact.food: Sparrows eat seeds, grains, and small insects.
fact.stronghold: Sparrows are common in many countries across Europe, Asia, Africa, and the Americas.
learningPoints: Their short beaks are well suited for cracking seeds.
Sparrows often travel and feed in flocks.
They can build nests in trees, roofs, and small gaps in buildings.

fact.title: Toucan
fact.fact: Toucans have very large, lightweight beaks that help them reach and peel fruit.
fact.food: Toucans eat fruit, insects, eggs, and small animals.
fact.stronghold: Toucans live in the rainforests of Central and South America.
learningPoints: Their colorful beaks also help them release body heat.
Toucans hop from branch to branch rather than walking on the ground.
They nest in tree holes.

fact.title: Arm
fact.fact: Your arms help you lift, reach, wave, hug, and carry many things every day.
fact.food: Strong arms are helped by healthy foods, water, and active play.
fact.stronghold: People around the world use their arms for work, sports, art, and daily activities.
learningPoints: Your arm includes the upper arm, elbow, forearm, wrist, and hand.
Muscles and bones work together to help the arm move.
Arms help with balance and movement too.

fact.title: Ear
fact.fact: Ears help us hear sounds and also help our bodies keep balance.
fact.food: Healthy eating and protecting ears from loud noise help them work well.
fact.stronghold: Ears are important body parts for people and many animals around the world.
learningPoints: The outer ear collects sound and sends it inward.
Tiny parts inside the ear help the brain understand sound.
The inner ear also helps you know if you are standing, turning, or moving.

fact.title: Eye
fact.fact: Eyes help us see colors, shapes, light, movement, and the world around us.
fact.food: Foods like carrots, leafy greens, and colorful fruits can help support eye health.
fact.stronghold: Eyes are one of the main sense organs used by people everywhere.
learningPoints: The pupil changes size to let more or less light in.
Your brain works with your eyes to build the picture you see.
Blinking helps keep your eyes moist and clean.

fact.title: Hair
fact.fact: Hair helps protect the head from sunlight and can also help keep the body warm.
fact.food: Healthy foods with protein and vitamins help hair grow strong.
fact.stronghold: People all around the world have different hair colors, textures, and styles.
learningPoints: Hair grows from tiny openings in the skin called follicles.
Hair does not feel pain when cut because the part above the skin is not alive.
Eyebrows and eyelashes are also types of hair that help protect the eyes.

fact.title: Hand
fact.fact: Hands help us hold, draw, clap, build, write, and explore many textures.
fact.food: Healthy meals and exercise help the muscles in your hands stay strong.
fact.stronghold: Hands are used every day by people all over the world for learning and play.
learningPoints: Each hand has fingers and a thumb that make gripping easier.
Hands have many small bones that allow careful movement.
Fingerprints on your hands are unique to you.

fact.title: Leg
fact.fact: Legs help us stand, walk, run, jump, dance, and climb.
fact.food: Bones and muscles in the legs are supported by movement and healthy foods.
fact.stronghold: People depend on their legs every day for travel, sports, and balance.
learningPoints: The thigh, knee, calf, ankle, and foot are all part of the leg area.
Leg muscles are some of the strongest muscles in the body.
Legs work with the feet to help the body stay balanced.

fact.title: Mouth
fact.fact: The mouth helps us eat, drink, speak, sing, and show expressions like smiling.
fact.food: Teeth and gums stay healthiest with water, balanced meals, and regular brushing.
fact.stronghold: Mouths are important for communication and eating in people around the world.
learningPoints: Teeth inside the mouth help bite and chew food.
The tongue helps with tasting and speaking.
Saliva helps soften food and keep the mouth moist.

fact.title: Neck
fact.fact: The neck supports the head and helps it turn, tilt, and look in different directions.
fact.food: Good posture, stretching, and healthy habits help keep the neck comfortable.
fact.stronghold: The neck is an important connecting part of the body for everyone.
learningPoints: The neck links the head to the shoulders and chest.
It contains bones, muscles, and the airway.
The neck helps protect important nerves and blood vessels.

fact.title: Nose
fact.fact: The nose helps us smell scents and also helps clean and warm the air we breathe.
fact.food: Smelling food with the nose helps the brain notice flavors and freshness.
fact.stronghold: People use their noses every day to breathe and detect smells all around the world.
learningPoints: Tiny sensors inside the nose help recognize many different smells.
The nose and tongue work together to help us enjoy flavor.
Nose hairs and mucus help trap dust before it enters the lungs.

images.title: Cheerful Garden Tapestry with Children Painting
Colorful Kite Flying in the Countryside
Cozy Twilight Storytime in the Garden
Enchanted Garden of Princesses and Roses
Autumn Harvest at Sunset with Children
Girls Reading in a Magical Meadow
Moonlit Garden with Castle and Flowers

fact.title: Apple
fact.fact: Apples grow on trees and come in many colors such as red, green, and yellow.
fact.food: Apples are fruits that people can eat fresh, cooked, or dried.
fact.stronghold: China is one of the world’s largest apple-growing countries.
learningPoints: Apple trees blossom with flowers before the fruit grows.
Apples contain fiber and vitamin C.
There are thousands of apple varieties around the world.

fact.title: Banana
fact.fact: Bananas grow in large hanging clusters and are picked while still green so they can ripen later.
fact.food: Bananas are soft, sweet fruits often eaten raw.
fact.stronghold: India is one of the biggest banana-producing countries in the world.
learningPoints: Banana plants are giant herbs, not trees.
Bananas are a good source of potassium.
The peel protects the fruit as it grows.

fact.title: Blueberry
fact.fact: Blueberries are small berries that often grow in clusters on shrubs.
fact.food: Blueberries are sweet fruits eaten fresh or baked into foods.
fact.stronghold: The United States and Canada are well known for blueberry production.
learningPoints: Blueberries start out green before turning blue.
They contain antioxidants that help protect cells.
Many birds also enjoy eating blueberries.

fact.title: Grapes
fact.fact: Grapes grow in bunches on vines and can be eaten fresh or dried into raisins.
fact.food: Grapes are sweet fruits that can be purple, red, or green.
fact.stronghold: China is one of the largest grape-growing countries in the world.
learningPoints: Grape vines need support as they grow.
Some grapes have seeds while others do not.
Raisins are dried grapes.

fact.title: Mango
fact.fact: Mangoes are tropical fruits known for their sweet taste and smooth orange-yellow flesh.
fact.food: Mangoes are eaten fresh and also used in drinks, salads, and desserts.
fact.stronghold: India is one of the biggest mango-producing countries in the world.
learningPoints: Each mango usually has one large seed in the center.
Mango trees can grow quite large.
Different mango varieties can taste a little different.

fact.title: Orange
fact.fact: Oranges are citrus fruits known for their juicy segments and fresh smell.
fact.food: Oranges are eaten fresh and also used for juice.
fact.stronghold: Brazil is one of the strongest orange-producing countries in the world.
learningPoints: Orange trees can keep green leaves all year long.
The fruit is rich in vitamin C.
Its thick peel helps protect the juicy inside.

fact.title: Apple
fact.fact: Apples grow on trees and come in many colors such as red, green, and yellow.
fact.food: Apples are fruits that people can eat fresh, cooked, or dried.
fact.stronghold: China is one of the world’s largest apple-growing countries.
learningPoints: Apple trees blossom with flowers before the fruit grows.
Apples contain fiber and vitamin C.
There are thousands of apple varieties around the world.

fact.title: Strawberry
fact.fact: Strawberries are unusual because their seeds are on the outside of the fruit.
fact.food: Strawberries are sweet fruits often eaten fresh or used in desserts.
fact.stronghold: China and the United States are among the major strawberry-producing countries.
learningPoints: A strawberry plant can spread using runners.
The bright red color helps attract animals that spread seeds.
Strawberries are a good source of vitamin C.

fact.title: Watermelon
fact.fact: Watermelons are large fruits with a lot of water inside, which helps them stay refreshing.
fact.food: Watermelon is eaten fresh and has sweet red or pink flesh.
fact.stronghold: China grows more watermelons than any other country.
learningPoints: Watermelons grow on sprawling vines along the ground.
They can have black seeds, pale seeds, or be seedless.
The rind protects the juicy center as it grows.

fact.title: Waterfall in a Misty Jungle
fact.fact: A waterfall forms where a river drops over a steep rock edge or cliff.
fact.food: This scene features falling water, misty air, lush plants, and a green valley.
fact.stronghold: Waterfalls are found worldwide, especially in mountainous and rainy regions.
learningPoints: Mist from waterfalls can help nearby plants stay moist.
Fast-moving water can slowly shape the rocks below.
Jungle valleys often support many kinds of plants and animals.

fact.title: Aurora Over Icy Lagoon
fact.fact: An aurora is a glowing light display in the sky created when particles from space interact with Earth’s atmosphere.
fact.food: This scene is known for colorful night skies, icy water, and reflections on the lagoon.
fact.stronghold: Auroras are most common near the Arctic and Antarctic regions, including Iceland, Norway, and Canada.
learningPoints: Auroras can appear green, pink, purple, and red.
They are easier to see on dark, clear nights.
The same natural event is called aurora borealis in the north and aurora australis in the south.

images.title: Stormy Waves on a Rugged Coast

fact.title: Stormy Waves on a Rugged Coast
fact.fact: Powerful ocean waves can crash against rocky shores and reshape coastlines over time.
fact.food: This scene features rough seas, dark clouds, and rocky cliffs along the shore.
fact.stronghold: Stormy coastlines are common in windy ocean regions such as the North Atlantic and the Southern Ocean.
learningPoints: Strong storms can make waves much larger than usual.
Rocky coasts are worn down little by little by water and wind.
Sea spray can travel far inland during rough weather.

fact.title: Glowing Mangrove Bay
fact.fact: Some bays glow at night because tiny living organisms in the water create bioluminescent light.
fact.food: This scene includes glowing water, mangrove trees, and a sky full of stars.
fact.stronghold: Bioluminescent bays are found in places such as Puerto Rico and some tropical coastal regions.
learningPoints: Mangroves grow along salty coasts and help protect shorelines.
Bioluminescence is light made by living things.
The glow often becomes brighter when the water is disturbed.

fact.title: Sunlight Through Canyon Walls
fact.fact: Canyons are deep valleys cut by rivers or floods over long periods of time.
fact.food: This scene shows narrow rock walls, warm sunlight beams, and smooth canyon curves.
fact.stronghold: Beautiful canyons are found in many dry regions, including the United States, Jordan, and Australia.
learningPoints: Sunlight can create glowing colors on canyon walls.
Flash floods can help carve narrow canyon passages.
Different rock layers can show Earth’s history over time.

images.title: Giant Trees in a Rocky Desert

fact.title: Giant Trees in a Rocky Desert
fact.fact: Some hardy trees can survive in dry places with little rain by storing water and growing deep roots.
fact.food: This scene shows giant trees, dry rocky ground, and wide open desert skies.
fact.stronghold: Tree-filled desert landscapes can be found in places such as Socotra, Namibia, and the American Southwest.
learningPoints: Desert plants often grow slowly and conserve water carefully.
Rocks and sand can become extremely hot during the day.
Even dry landscapes can support unique life forms.

fact.title: Sandstone Pillars in Mist
fact.fact: Sandstone pillars are tall rock formations shaped over time by wind, rain, and changing temperatures.
fact.food: This scene shows steep rock pillars, misty air, and a green forested valley.
fact.stronghold: Towering sandstone formations are found in places such as China, the United States, and parts of Europe.
learningPoints: Sandstone forms from layers of sand pressed together over a very long time.
Mist can make distant cliffs look softer and more mysterious.
Erosion slowly changes rock shapes over thousands of years.

fact.title: Geothermal Springs
fact.fact: Geothermal springs are heated by warm energy from deep inside the Earth.
fact.food: This scene features steaming water, mineral-rich pools, and surrounding forest views.
fact.stronghold: Geothermal springs are common in places with volcanic activity, such as Iceland, New Zealand, and Yellowstone in the United States.
learningPoints: Some springs bubble because hot water and gases rise to the surface.
Minerals can color the water and rocks in surprising shades.
These areas can be very hot, so people should stay on safe paths.

fact.title: Mirror of the Fiery Sky
fact.fact: Still water can reflect a colorful sunrise or sunset so clearly that it looks like a mirror.
fact.food: This scene is known for calm water, dramatic sky colors, and bright reflections.
fact.stronghold: These reflections can happen anywhere there is still water and a vivid sky.
learningPoints: Red and orange skies often appear when sunlight travels through more of the atmosphere.
Wind can break up a reflection by making waves.
Reflections are easier to see on smooth water than on rough water.

fact.title: Cascading Terraces
fact.fact: Terraces are step-like levels built on hillsides to manage water and make farming easier.
fact.food: This scene is known for layered slopes, shining water, and warm golden tones.
fact.stronghold: Terraced landscapes are found in countries such as China, Vietnam, Peru, and the Philippines.
learningPoints: Terraces help keep soil from washing away.
They can hold water for crops like rice.
Sunlight can make terraced fields glow beautifully at sunrise or sunset.

fact.title: Zero
fact.fact: Zero means none at all, and it is an important number in math and counting.
fact.food: You can use zero when talking about food, like zero cookies left on the plate.
fact.stronghold: Zero is used in number systems all around the world.
learningPoints: Zero acts as a placeholder in larger numbers like 10 and 100.
It helps show that nothing is in a group.
Adding zero to a number does not change the number.

fact.title: One
fact.fact: One is the first counting number and means a single thing.
fact.food: You can count one apple, one carrot, or one slice of bread.
fact.stronghold: The number one is used in counting systems everywhere.
learningPoints: One stands for a single object or idea.
It is the building block for larger numbers.
When you add one, the count grows by a single step.

fact.title: Two
fact.fact: Two means a pair, like two shoes or two eyes.
fact.food: You might have two bananas or two peas in a small group.
fact.stronghold: Two is a basic number used worldwide in counting and measuring.
learningPoints: Two things together can make a pair.
Many animals and people have two eyes and two ears.
Two is an even number.

fact.title: Three
fact.fact: Three is often seen in groups, like traffic-light colors or the sides of a triangle.
fact.food: You can count three grapes, three peas, or three cookies.
fact.stronghold: Three is used around the world in counting, shapes, and patterns.
learningPoints: A triangle has three sides.
Three is an odd number.
Many stories and songs use groups of three because they are easy to remember.

fact.title: Four
fact.fact: Four is common in nature and everyday life, like four seasons or four legs on many animals.
fact.food: A child might count four strawberries or four slices of cucumber.
fact.stronghold: Four is a widely used counting number across the world.
learningPoints: A square has four sides.
Many tables and chairs stand on four legs.
Four is an even number.

fact.title: Five
fact.fact: Five is easy to spot because many people have five fingers on each hand.
fact.food: You can count five blueberries or five pieces of popcorn.
fact.stronghold: Five is used worldwide in counting, games, and measuring.
learningPoints: Hands make five easy to learn by counting fingers.
Five is an odd number.
Many star shapes are drawn with five points.

fact.title: Six
fact.fact: Six appears in nature too, such as the six legs of an insect.
fact.food: A snack bowl could hold six grapes or six crackers.
fact.stronghold: Six is used around the world in counting and grouping.
learningPoints: Many insects have six legs.
Six is an even number.
A hexagon has six sides.

fact.title: Seven
fact.fact: Seven is a number often remembered in songs, stories, and the days of the week.
fact.food: A child could count seven peas or seven orange slices.
fact.stronghold: Seven is a familiar counting number used globally.
learningPoints: There are seven days in a week.
Seven is an odd number.
It is often seen as a special number in stories and games.

fact.title: Eight
fact.fact: Eight can be seen in nature, like the eight legs of an octopus or spider.
fact.food: You might count eight raisins or eight berries in a snack.
fact.stronghold: Eight is used in counting and math all over the world.
learningPoints: An octopus has eight arms.
Eight is an even number.
When turned sideways, 8 looks like the infinity symbol.

fact.title: Nine
fact.fact: Nine is one step before ten and helps children practice bigger number groups.
fact.food: You can count nine seeds, nine peas, or nine crackers.
fact.stronghold: Nine is a standard counting number used worldwide.
learningPoints: Nine is an odd number.
It comes right before ten.
Three groups of three make nine.

fact.title: Clownfish
fact.fact: Clownfish are small, bright fish famous for living safely among the stinging tentacles of sea anemones.
fact.food: Clownfish eat algae, plankton, and tiny sea animals.
fact.stronghold: Clownfish live in warm waters of the Indian and Pacific Oceans.
learningPoints: A special mucus on their skin helps protect them from anemone stings.
They are usually orange with white stripes.
Clownfish and sea anemones help each other survive.

fact.title: Dolphin
fact.fact: Dolphins are smart marine mammals that breathe air and often live in social groups called pods.
fact.food: Dolphins eat fish, squid, and other small sea animals.
fact.stronghold: Many dolphins live in warm and temperate oceans around the world, with large populations in the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans.
learningPoints: Dolphins come to the surface to breathe through a blowhole.
They use clicks and whistles to communicate and find food.
Dolphins are mammals, so mothers feed milk to their babies.

fact.title: Jellyfish
fact.fact: Jellyfish have soft, umbrella-shaped bodies and trailing tentacles that drift through the water.
fact.food: Jellyfish eat tiny fish, plankton, and other small sea creatures.
fact.stronghold: Jellyfish live in oceans all over the world, from shallow coasts to deep sea waters.
learningPoints: Some jellyfish glow in the dark ocean with bioluminescence.
Their tentacles can sting to help catch food.
Jellyfish have no bones, brain, or heart.

fact.title: Lionfish
fact.fact: Lionfish are striking reef fish with long, fan-like spines and bold stripes.
fact.food: Lionfish eat small fish and crustaceans.
fact.stronghold: Lionfish are native to the Indo-Pacific region, though they are also found in other waters as an invasive species.
learningPoints: Their spines contain venom for defense.
Lionfish often move slowly and hunt by surprising prey.
Their dramatic fins help them look larger to predators.

fact.title: Manta Ray
fact.fact: Manta rays are large, graceful sea animals with wide fins that look like underwater wings.
fact.food: Manta rays mainly eat plankton and tiny sea animals.
fact.stronghold: Manta rays are found in tropical and subtropical oceans around the world.
learningPoints: They glide through the water with smooth, powerful movements.
Manta rays do not have the stinging tail seen in some other rays.
They often visit coral reefs and cleaning stations.

fact.title: Octopus
fact.fact: Octopuses are clever sea animals with eight arms and strong suckers.
fact.food: Octopuses eat crabs, shellfish, and other small sea creatures.
fact.stronghold: Octopuses live in oceans all over the world, often hiding in reefs and rocky seafloors.
learningPoints: An octopus can squeeze through very small spaces.
It can change color to help hide from predators.
Octopuses are known for problem-solving skills.

fact.title: Sea Turtle
fact.fact: Sea turtles are reptiles that spend most of their lives in the ocean but come onto beaches to lay eggs.
fact.food: Different sea turtles eat seagrass, jellyfish, crabs, and other sea life.
fact.stronghold: Sea turtles live in warm and temperate oceans, with important nesting beaches in countries such as Australia, Costa Rica, and Oman.
learningPoints: Sea turtles can travel very long distances in the ocean.
Their flippers help them swim strongly through the water.
Many baby turtles hatch on beaches and head to the sea on their own.

fact.title: Seahorse
fact.fact: Seahorses are tiny fish that swim upright and use their curled tails to hold onto plants or coral.
fact.food: Seahorses eat tiny shrimp-like animals and plankton.
fact.stronghold: Seahorses live in shallow tropical and temperate waters around the world.
learningPoints: A seahorse uses its long snout like a straw to suck up food.
Male seahorses carry the babies in a pouch.
They are not strong swimmers and often drift with the water.

fact.title: Starfish
fact.fact: Starfish, also called sea stars, are marine animals that usually have five arms.
fact.food: Sea stars often eat shellfish and tiny sea animals.
fact.stronghold: Sea stars live in oceans worldwide, from coral reefs to rocky shores.
learningPoints: A sea star can move using tiny tube feet underneath its body.
Some sea stars can regrow a lost arm.
They are not actually fish.

fact.title: Whale
fact.fact: Whales are giant marine mammals that travel through oceans and come to the surface to breathe air.
fact.food: Different whales eat krill, plankton, fish, or squid depending on the species.
fact.stronghold: Whales are found in every ocean, with many species migrating across huge distances each year.
learningPoints: Some whales are the largest animals on Earth.
Whales use sounds to communicate over long distances underwater.
Like all mammals, whales breathe air and care for their young.

fact.title: Whale
fact.fact: Whales are giant marine mammals that travel through oceans and come to the surface to breathe air.
fact.food: Different whales eat krill, plankton, fish, or squid depending on the species.
fact.stronghold: Whales are found in every ocean, with many species migrating across huge distances each year.
learningPoints: Some whales are the largest animals on Earth.
Whales use sounds to communicate over long distances underwater.
Like all mammals, whales breathe air and care for their young.

fact.title: Circle
fact.fact: A circle is a round shape with no corners and no straight sides.
fact.food: Many foods look like circles, such as orange slices, pancakes, and cookies.
fact.stronghold: Circles are seen everywhere in signs, wheels, clocks, and toys around the world.
learningPoints: Every point around a circle is the same distance from the center.
A circle can roll smoothly because it has no corners.
Balls, coins, and plates often show circular shapes.

fact.title: Diamond
fact.fact: A diamond shape looks like a square tilted onto one corner.
fact.food: Some crackers, candies, and decorations are cut into diamond shapes.
fact.stronghold: Diamond shapes appear in patterns, road signs, kites, and art around the world.
learningPoints: A diamond shape has four sides.
It can look narrow or wide depending on how it is drawn.
Many kites use a diamond-like outline.

fact.title: Heart
fact.fact: The heart shape is a symbol often used for love, care, and kindness.
fact.food: Heart shapes are popular in cookies, sandwiches, candies, and decorations.
fact.stronghold: Heart symbols are recognized in many countries and cultures.
learningPoints: The heart symbol is different from the real body organ, but both are important symbols.
It has a pointed bottom and rounded top sections.
It is often seen on cards and gifts.

fact.title: Hexagon
fact.fact: A hexagon is a shape with six sides.
fact.food: Some honeycomb candies and crackers can be shaped like hexagons.
fact.stronghold: Hexagons appear in nature and design, especially in beehives.
learningPoints: A honeycomb is made from many hexagons fitted together.
Six equal sides make a regular hexagon.
Hexagons can pack tightly without leaving gaps.

fact.title: Oval
fact.fact: An oval is a rounded shape that looks stretched compared with a circle.
fact.food: Eggs, some fruits, and many plates or decorations can be oval-shaped.
fact.stronghold: Ovals are common in sports tracks, mirrors, and everyday design.
learningPoints: An oval has no corners.
It is longer in one direction than the other.
A circle can be thought of as a special kind of round shape, while an oval is more stretched.

fact.title: Pentagon
fact.fact: A pentagon is a shape with five sides.
fact.food: Some cookies, tiles, and craft cutouts can be made in pentagon shapes.
fact.stronghold: Pentagons appear in geometry, art, and building patterns.
learningPoints: A regular pentagon has five equal sides.
Many stars can be drawn inside a pentagon.
Five corners make the pentagon easy to recognize.

fact.title: Rectangle
fact.fact: A rectangle is a four-sided shape with opposite sides the same length.
fact.food: Sandwiches, chocolate bars, and crackers are often cut into rectangles.
fact.stronghold: Rectangles are one of the most common shapes in books, doors, screens, and windows everywhere.
learningPoints: A rectangle has four right-angle corners.
Squares are a special type of rectangle.
Many buildings and objects use rectangles because they stack neatly.

fact.title: Square
fact.fact: A square is a four-sided shape with all sides the same length.
fact.food: Cheese slices, crackers, and brownies are often made in square shapes.
fact.stronghold: Squares are used in games, floor tiles, blocks, and picture grids all around the world.
learningPoints: A square has four equal sides and four equal corners.
It looks the same even when turned.
Many puzzle boards and game boards use square sections.

fact.title: Star
fact.fact: A star shape usually has points that stretch outward from the center.
fact.food: Cookies, fruit slices, and sandwiches are sometimes cut into star shapes.
fact.stronghold: Star shapes are popular in flags, decorations, stickers, and holiday crafts around the world.
learningPoints: Many simple stars have five points.
Stars can be drawn in different styles with different numbers of points.
The night sky inspired many star-shaped symbols.

fact.title: Triangle
fact.fact: A triangle is a shape with three sides and three corners.
fact.food: Pizza slices, nachos, and sandwiches are often cut into triangles.
fact.stronghold: Triangles appear in road signs, roofs, art, and structures all over the world.
learningPoints: A triangle is one of the strongest simple shapes in building.
All triangles have exactly three sides.
Some triangles have equal sides while others do not.

fact.title: Asteroid
fact.fact: Asteroids are rocky objects that orbit the Sun, and many are smaller than planets.
fact.food: They are chunks of rock and metal left over from the early solar system.
fact.stronghold: Many asteroids are found in the asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter.
learningPoints: Asteroids come in many sizes and shapes.
Some asteroids have tiny moons of their own.
Scientists study asteroids to learn about the early solar system.

fact.title: Earth
fact.fact: Earth is the only known planet with liquid water on the surface and life all around it.
fact.food: It is a rocky planet with oceans, land, clouds, and an atmosphere we can breathe.
fact.stronghold: Earth is the third planet from the Sun.
learningPoints: About 70 percent of Earth’s surface is covered by water.
Earth has one natural moon.
Its atmosphere helps protect life from harmful radiation.

fact.title: Jupiter
fact.fact: Jupiter is the largest planet in our solar system.
fact.food: It is a giant gas planet with swirling clouds and a huge storm called the Great Red Spot.
fact.stronghold: Jupiter is the fifth planet from the Sun.
learningPoints: Jupiter has many moons.
It is made mostly of hydrogen and helium.
Its Great Red Spot is a giant storm larger than Earth.

fact.title: Mars
fact.fact: Mars is often called the Red Planet because iron-rich dust gives it a reddish color.
fact.food: It is a cold rocky planet with volcanoes, canyons, and polar ice caps.
fact.stronghold: Mars is the fourth planet from the Sun.
learningPoints: Mars has two small moons.
Scientists study Mars for signs of ancient water.
Some of the tallest volcanoes in the solar system are on Mars.

fact.title: Mercury
fact.fact: Mercury is the closest planet to the Sun and has a rocky surface full of craters.
fact.food: It is a small rocky planet with almost no atmosphere to hold in heat.
fact.stronghold: Mercury is the first planet from the Sun.
learningPoints: Mercury has very hot days and very cold nights.
It has no moons.
A year on Mercury is much shorter than a year on Earth.

fact.title: Neptune
fact.fact: Neptune is a deep blue icy giant and one of the windiest places in the solar system.
fact.food: It is a cold outer planet with fast-moving storms and a thick atmosphere.
fact.stronghold: Neptune is the eighth planet from the Sun.
learningPoints: Neptune is very far from the Sun.
It has powerful winds and dark storm systems.
Methane in the atmosphere contributes to its blue color.

fact.title: Saturn
fact.fact: Saturn is famous for its bright rings made of ice and rock.
fact.food: It is a giant gas planet with pale cloud bands and a large ring system.
fact.stronghold: Saturn is the sixth planet from the Sun.
learningPoints: Saturn’s rings are wide but very thin.
It has many moons, including Titan.
Like Jupiter, Saturn is made mostly of hydrogen and helium.

fact.title: Sun
fact.fact: The Sun is the star at the center of our solar system and gives Earth light and warmth.
fact.food: It is a huge glowing ball of hot gases, mostly hydrogen and helium.
fact.stronghold: The Sun is at the center of the solar system, and all the planets travel around it.
learningPoints: Plants use sunlight to make food.
The Sun is much larger than Earth.
Without the Sun, Earth would be dark and freezing cold.

fact.title: Uranus
fact.fact: Uranus is an icy giant planet that rotates tipped over on its side.
fact.food: It is a cold blue-green planet with a thick atmosphere and faint rings.
fact.stronghold: Uranus is the seventh planet from the Sun.
learningPoints: Methane in its atmosphere helps give Uranus its blue-green color.
Its unusual tilt makes its seasons very extreme.
Uranus is much farther from the Sun than Earth.

fact.title: Venus
fact.fact: Venus is a rocky planet covered by thick clouds that trap a great deal of heat.
fact.food: It is often called Earth’s sister planet because it is similar in size.
fact.stronghold: Venus is the second planet from the Sun.
learningPoints: Venus is one of the hottest planets in the solar system.
Its thick clouds are made mostly of carbon dioxide with acid clouds high above.
Venus spins very slowly compared with Earth.

fact.title: Cloudy
fact.fact: Cloudy weather means most of the sky is covered by clouds, which can block a lot of sunlight.
fact.food: Soups, sandwiches, and warm snacks often feel nice on cloudy days.
fact.stronghold: Cloudy weather is common in many temperate and coastal places around the world.
learningPoints: Clouds can help keep temperatures cooler during the day.
Some clouds bring rain, while others just drift by.
The sky can still be bright even when the Sun is hidden by clouds.

fact.title: Foggy
fact.fact: Fog is a cloud that forms very close to the ground, making it harder to see far away.
fact.food: Warm drinks and hot breakfasts can feel extra cozy on foggy mornings.
fact.stronghold: Fog is common in places with cool air and moisture, such as coastal California and mountain valleys.
learningPoints: Fog forms when water vapor in the air cools and condenses.
Drivers should slow down and use extra care in fog.
Fog often clears when the Sun warms the air.

fact.title: Hot
fact.fact: Hot weather means the air temperature is high and can make the body warm quickly.
fact.food: People often choose cold water, fruit, and chilled foods in hot weather.
fact.stronghold: Very hot weather is common in desert regions such as the Sahara and parts of the Middle East.
learningPoints: Drinking water helps the body stay cool in hot weather.
Shade can make a big difference on a hot day.
Animals often rest more during the hottest part of the day.

fact.title: Partly Cloudy
fact.fact: Partly cloudy weather means some of the sky is covered with clouds while sunlight still peeks through.
fact.food: It is a comfortable kind of weather for picnics, fruit snacks, and outdoor meals.
fact.stronghold: Partly cloudy skies can happen almost anywhere as clouds move in and out during the day.
learningPoints: Clouds are made of tiny water droplets or ice crystals.
Partly cloudy days can feel cooler than fully sunny days.
Changing cloud cover can be a sign that the weather is shifting.

fact.title: Rainbow
fact.fact: A rainbow appears when sunlight passes through raindrops and bends into many colors.
fact.food: Rainbows do not change what people eat, but they often appear after rainy weather clears.
fact.stronghold: Rainbows can appear anywhere in the world when sunlight and raindrops are in the right position.
learningPoints: The colors usually appear in the order red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, and violet.
You need both sunlight and water droplets to see a rainbow.
A rainbow is an optical effect, not a solid object in the sky.

fact.title: Rainy
fact.fact: Rainy weather happens when clouds release drops of water that fall to the ground.
fact.food: Rainy days often make people want warm meals, tea, or soup indoors.
fact.stronghold: Rainy weather is especially common in tropical rainforests and monsoon regions such as India and Southeast Asia.
learningPoints: Rain helps refill rivers, lakes, and underground water supplies.
Plants and crops need rain to grow.
Puddles form when the ground cannot soak up all the water right away.

fact.title: Snowy
fact.fact: Snow falls when water in clouds freezes into ice crystals and drops to the ground.
fact.food: Hot soup, cocoa, and warm meals are popular on snowy days.
fact.stronghold: Snowy weather is common in colder countries such as Canada, Norway, and parts of Japan.
learningPoints: Every snowflake forms from ice crystals in the clouds.
Snow can help keep the ground insulated and warmer underneath.
Snowy weather usually happens when the air is cold enough for ice to stay frozen.

fact.title: Sunny
fact.fact: Sunny weather happens when the sky is mostly clear and sunlight reaches the ground directly.
fact.food: People often enjoy cool water, juicy fruit, and light foods on sunny days.
fact.stronghold: Sunny weather is common in many dry regions, including parts of Egypt, Australia, and the southwestern United States.
learningPoints: The Sun helps warm the land, air, and water.
Wearing sunscreen and a hat can help protect skin on bright days.
Plants use sunlight to make their own food.

fact.title: Thunderstorm
fact.fact: A thunderstorm is a storm with thunder, lightning, dark clouds, and often heavy rain.
fact.food: Many people like staying indoors with warm snacks while thunderstorms pass.
fact.stronghold: Thunderstorms happen in many places, especially in warm, humid regions such as Florida and parts of central Africa.
learningPoints: Lightning is a giant electrical spark in the atmosphere.
Thunder is the sound made when lightning heats the air very quickly.
It is safest to go indoors when thunder is heard.

fact.title: Windy
fact.fact: Windy weather happens when air moves quickly from one place to another.
fact.food: On windy days, people often enjoy warm foods and drinks after playing outside.
fact.stronghold: Very windy conditions are common in open plains, mountain passes, and coastal areas.
learningPoints: Wind can move clouds, seeds, and even help sailboats travel.
Kites fly best when there is enough wind.
Strong winds can make the air feel colder than the thermometer says.

fact.title: Christ the Redeemer
fact.fact: Christ the Redeemer is a giant statue standing with open arms above Rio de Janeiro.
fact.food: It is famous for its mountaintop location, sweeping city views, and welcoming pose.
fact.stronghold: The statue stands on Corcovado Mountain in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil.
learningPoints: It is one of the most recognized statues in the world.
The statue overlooks the city and coastline below.
It is considered one of the New Seven Wonders of the World.

fact.title: Colosseum
fact.fact: The Colosseum is a giant ancient amphitheater where Romans once gathered for public events.
fact.food: It is famous for its huge arches, layered seating, and ancient engineering.
fact.stronghold: The Colosseum is in Rome, Italy.
learningPoints: It could hold tens of thousands of people.
It is one of the best-known buildings from ancient Rome.
Parts of it still stand after many centuries.

fact.title: Great Pyramid of Giza
fact.fact: The Great Pyramid of Giza is the oldest and largest pyramid in the famous pyramid fields of Egypt.
fact.food: It is famous for huge stone blocks, ancient engineering, and lasting for thousands of years.
fact.stronghold: The Great Pyramid of Giza stands near Cairo in Egypt.
learningPoints: It was built as a tomb for an Egyptian pharaoh.
For a very long time, it was the tallest human-made structure on Earth.
It is the last surviving wonder of the ancient world.

fact.title: Great Wall of China
fact.fact: The Great Wall is a huge system of walls and watchtowers built over many centuries in China.
fact.food: It is famous for its long stone pathways, mountain views, and history of guarding ancient borders.
fact.stronghold: The Great Wall is in northern China, with famous restored sections near Beijing.
learningPoints: It is one of the most famous landmarks in the world.
Different dynasties added new parts over time.
Some sections climb steep hills and mountains.

fact.title: Machu Picchu
fact.fact: Machu Picchu is an ancient Inca site high in the Andes Mountains.
fact.food: It is famous for its stone terraces, mountain setting, and Inca history.
fact.stronghold: Machu Picchu is in Peru near Cusco.
learningPoints: The site sits high above a river valley.
The Inca built strong stone walls without modern machines.
It stayed hidden from much of the outside world for a long time.

fact.title: Petra
fact.fact: Petra is an ancient city famous for buildings carved directly into pink rock cliffs.
fact.food: It is famous for rock-cut architecture, narrow canyons, and desert history.
fact.stronghold: Petra is in southern Jordan.
learningPoints: Petra is sometimes called the Rose City because of its stone color.
People enter through a narrow canyon called the Siq.
It was an important trading city long ago.

fact.title: Taj Mahal
fact.fact: The Taj Mahal is a white marble monument built in India and known for its beautiful symmetry.
fact.food: It is famous for its shining marble, detailed carvings, and reflecting pools.
fact.stronghold: The Taj Mahal stands in Agra, India, on the banks of the Yamuna River.
learningPoints: It was built as a mausoleum.
The marble can look different colors at different times of day.
It is one of the New Seven Wonders of the World.
